overview: Living End is a four-color combo deck that aims to cast cheap spells to trigger its namesake card, exiling the entire board and returning creatures with 0+ mana value to create an overwhelming advantage. It relies on interaction like Force of Negation and Subtlety to protect its combo pieces and control the game until the trigger resolves. The deck operates in Modern by leveraging a wide range of cheap instants and sorceries to reliably reach the necessary trigger count.
win_conditions: Triggering Living End to exile all permanents and return creatures with 0+ mana value to the battlefield
Establishing a board state with multiple low-cost creatures that outvalue the opponent
Using interaction to stall until the combo can be assembled and resolved

keep_criteria: Hands with at least two cheap instants or sorceries
Hands containing Living End plus a trigger source
Hands with early interaction like Force of Negation or Subtlety
mulligan_criteria: Hands missing interaction or combo pieces

early_game: Play cheap lands and trigger sources to build toward the Living End trigger
Use Force of Negation to counter key threats or protect your combo pieces
Hold Subtlety to silence problematic creatures or prevent opponent's interaction
mid_game: Continue casting cheap spells to reach the required trigger count for Living End
Maintain hand size and card advantage by cycling or drawing into more triggers
Use interaction to clear the path for your combo resolution
late_game: Cast Living End once you have enough triggers queued or in hand
Return a suite of low-cost creatures to dominate the board
Stall with remaining interaction if the combo is disrupted

key_cards.name: Living End
key_cards.role: Core combo piece that exiles all permanents and returns creatures with 0+ mana value
key_cards.notes: Cast only when you have enough cheap spells cast that turn or can queue them in the same turn to maximize the board reflood

key_cards.name: Force of Negation
key_cards.role: Priority 0 counter that protects combo pieces or shuts down threats
key_cards.notes: Use to counter opponent's removal or counterspells targeting your Living End or trigger sources | Used to counter Cord of Calling and Solitude to deal with Nadu while it is on the stack. (from gameplay) | Used to counter key combo pieces on the stack to prevent the opponent from drawing cards, serving as the primary interaction against the combo. (from gameplay)

key_cards.name: Subtlety
key_cards.role: Silence effect that prevents creatures from activating abilities or attacking
key_cards.notes: Hold to shut down problematic threats or use to protect your low-cost creatures from being removed by activated abilities | Mentioned as a card that counters can't help against in games 1 and 2, indicating it bypasses standard counter-magic interaction. (from gameplay) | Bypasses certain stack-based protections in games one and two, indicating it requires direct answers rather than relying on stack manipulation effects. (from gameplay)

sequencing_tips: Cast your cheapest instants and sorceries before Living End to ensure the trigger count is met
Hold interaction in hand to respond to opponent's attempts to disrupt your combo
Queue spells in the same turn as Living End to maximize the number of creatures returned

matchups.advice: Prioritize early interaction and hold Subtlety to stall until you can trigger Living End
watch_for: Pressure that forces you to play defensively

matchups.advice: Use Force of Negation to protect your combo pieces and cycle through your deck to find triggers
Balance trigger casting with interaction to avoid being outvalued before your combo resolves

Use Force of Negation to counter Cord of Calling and Solitude to counter Nadu while it is on the stack, preventing the opponent from drawing cards instead. (from gameplay) Include Dress Down to temporarily remove Nadu's abilities, disrupting its combo potential for a turn. (from gameplay) Sun Cleanser is highly effective against the energy package (Galvanic Discharge, Wrath of the Skies) because it removes all energy counters and prevents the opponent from gaining new ones. (from gameplay) The control list is specifically tuned to beat Bant Nadu, providing a strong advantage in games 1 and 2 before sideboard adjustments. (from gameplay) Prioritize countering key combo pieces on the stack with Force of Negation rather than playing board wipes that allow the opponent to draw cards instead. (from gameplay) Be aware that certain stack-protection effects do not help resolve key combo pieces against Subtlety in games one and two, requiring direct answers rather than stack manipulation. (from gameplay)
